title: Macaranga keyensis
intro: Macaranga keyensis, also known as Key's macaranga, is a species of plant in the family Euphorbiaceae, commonly found in tropical forests of Malaysia and Southeast Asia. Traditionally, Macaranga keyensis has been used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to its potential health benefits of anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Macaranga keyensis include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Macaranga keyensis is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is commonly used in traditional remedies for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects.
taxonomy: Macaranga keyensis belongs to the family Euphorbiaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the spurge family. The genus Macaranga is a group of tropical trees and shrubs, and within it, M. keyensis is a specific species. This species is native to New Guinea and is often found in tropical rainforests. The classification of M. keyensis can be broken down further into its rank as a species within the kingdom Plantae. The classification is: kingdom Plantae, clade Angiosperms, clade Eudicots, clade Rosids, clade Malpighiales, family Euphorbiaceae, genus Macaranga, species M. keyensis.
